name: Toyol
description: The Toyol is a mischievous and malevolent spirit in Malay folklore, often depicted as a small, childlike being created through black magic. It is believed to be raised from a stillborn infant or a prematurely deceased child, animated by a bomoh (shaman or witch doctor) for the purpose of serving its master. Toyols are typically used to steal money, valuables, or information for their owners. They are said to be incredibly fast and difficult to catch, making them effective thieves. However, they are also easily distracted by simple things like toys or shiny objects. The creation and control of a Toyol is considered a dangerous and unethical practice, often associated with negative consequences for the owner. They are a symbol of greed, exploitation, and the misuse of supernatural powers. The Toyol's insatiable desire for shiny objects or small items makes them a constant threat to households, and countermeasures are often employed to protect against their mischievous nature. They are a persistent reminder of the dark aspects of magic and the potential for its abuse.
subtitle: Mischievous spirit of stolen goods
appearance: The Toyol is typically described as a small, naked humanoid figure resembling a baby or toddler. Its skin is often depicted as pale, greenish, or greyish, and it may have sharp teeth or claws. Some accounts portray it as having large, piercing eyes and a bald head. Its appearance can vary depending on the region and the specific ritual used to create it.
habitat: Toyols are not tied to a specific geographical location but rather to their masters. They are typically found in and around the homes or businesses of those who control them. They can also be found lurking in dark corners, abandoned buildings, or other places where they can hide and observe their surroundings.
behavior: Toyols are inherently mischievous and greedy. They are driven by an insatiable desire to steal and gather items for their masters. They are easily distracted by toys, shiny objects, or anything that catches their attention. Toyols are obedient to their masters but can be difficult to control completely, often engaging in minor acts of mischief even without direct instruction. They are generally considered to be malevolent spirits, capable of causing harm to those who cross them or their masters.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of a Toyol
keyMyths.description: A Toyol is created through a dark ritual involving the corpse of a stillborn or prematurely deceased child. The bomoh uses black magic to animate the body, binding it to their will.

keyMyths.title: Protecting Against Toyols
keyMyths.description: Households often place items like needles, beans, or mirrors near entrances to deter Toyols. Religious prayers and symbols are also used as protection.

sources.author: Rosemary Ellen Guiley
sources.work: The Encyclopedia of Demons and Demonology
sources.description: Provides a detailed overview of various demonic entities, including those found in Southeast Asian folklore.
sources.text: Provides a detailed overview of various demonic entities, including those found in Southeast Asian folklore.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Oral traditions and written accounts passed down through generations in Malaysia and surrounding regions.
